Fear has a distinct effect in the body. The body contracts, muscles, lungs, veins, arteries. But the heart beat goes up, which means the blood pumps out of the heart fast, but finds it hard to move forward in blood vessels due to the contraction. That is how Heart related issues emerge. 
Secondly, whenever the effect of fear starts to wear out, starts to become lighter- thinner, starts to become shallow, the urge to watch something fearful emerges in the body. That craving to feel fear is the reason why humans spend great deal of money to replenish the effect of fear within the body. And we do so delightfully. How foolish we are. And then we say, we want to feel no fear and Anxiety in our day to day life. 
We make ourselves miserable. 😂😂

$38 million?

Why?

I mean, I’m intrigued and all but why did this horror film cost $38,000,000?

It’s as though these “new masters of horror” can’t budget?

Halloween cost $300,000 in 78.

It made $70,000,000.

Texas Chainsaw Massacre in 74 had a budget of $80,000 - $100,000 yet made $38,000,000.

Seems to me that there are far more diminished returns the more these movies cost to make.

Barbarian made 10x its budget back.

Halloween in 1978 made 225x its budget back.


See what I mean?


Granted, Get Out did extraordinarily well given its budget but Peele’s next 2 movies have made LESS money and COST more to make.

Cregger now has 10x the budget of Barbarian.

It needs to make BIG bucks to prove itself.

At least $150,000,000.

Still only a 3x return on cost.

Prediction of the movie based on Chat GPT response

Alright, based on typical horror-thriller storytelling patterns, Zach Cregger’s style from Barbarian, and the clues from the trailer, here’s a grounded prediction:

Weapons is likely a supernatural or cosmic horror story disguised as a mystery drama. At the surface, it seems like a mass disappearance story, but underneath, I think it’s about a hidden force—maybe an ancient ritual, curse, or entity—that uses children as "weapons" to punish or expose the sins of adults in the town.

Here’s a realistic scenario: the town has a dark history it’s buried—maybe a tragic cover-up involving the school or the kids’ families. The children’s disappearance is supernatural justice or revenge triggered at 2:17 a.m. for a reason tied to that buried truth. One child is left behind—Alex—possibly because he’s either a witness, the key to the mystery, or even unknowingly involved. Each character’s storyline (like the teacher and the grieving father) peels back part of the mystery, revealing that the horror isn’t just about the kids being gone—it’s about why they were taken and what the town has tried to hide.

Kind of like The Mist meets The Leftovers, with that Magnolia-style emotional unraveling.


The town of Maybrook probably has a deeply buried, shameful secret—maybe something like a failed experiment, occult ritual, or a traumatic abuse scandal that involved children in the past. Think of something the town either collectively ignored or actively covered up. Over time, they moved on like it never happened—but trauma doesn't disappear. In stories like this, the "supernatural event" is often the consequence of the past clawing its way back into the present.

Example scenario: Back in the 90s, the school was involved in something horrific—maybe illegal testing tied to a government or private entity, masked as a learning program. The town turned a blind eye because it benefited them (funding, prestige, etc.). Years later, the new class (the 17 kids) starts exhibiting odd behaviors—maybe they draw the same images, speak of the same dreams, or become emotionally distant. The teacher (Julia Garner's character) thinks it's trauma-related, but it’s deeper—something’s waking up.

At 2:17 a.m.—the exact time when the original event happened years ago—the kids vanish. That time isn’t random. It’s symbolic. And the one child left behind? He’s not special in a savior way—he’s a message. Maybe he saw what happened, or maybe he’s possessed by the collective voices of the vanished children.

As the teacher and grieving father dig, they realize that this isn’t about finding the kids—it’s about facing what they allowed to happen. The “weapons” aren’t the kids literally—they’re metaphors for how past sins, when buried, will explode eventually.

I wouldn’t be surprised if the ending leaves us with no full closure—maybe the kids don’t come back, or maybe they do but are changed. The town's reality is shattered, and the survivors are left to wrestle with the fact that they were part of a system that created this outcome.

The name Matthew is shown prominently twice in this trailer. The kids woke up and left at 2:17am. In the Bible, Matthew 2:16-18 says this: "16When Herod saw that he had been outwitted by the Magi, he was filled with rage. Sending orders, he put to death all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under, according to the time he had learned from the Magi. 17Then what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah was fulfilled: 18“A voice is heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children, and refusing to be comforted, because they are no more.”…"
In context it refers to the "slaughter of infants" but seeing as these kids are older, there must be a different angle to it.

Human Rights Lawyer David Matas

Mr. David Matas addresses the audience at the film screening.

After Mr. Matas’s presentation, some audience members asked what could be done to stop the forced organ harvesting. Mr. Matas responded that medical technology was developed to improve people’s health and living standards, but in China it has been abused and used as a tool for large-scale human rights violations and mass killings. He emphasized that in response to this problem, the medical industry and people from all walks of life have begun to take action.

New legislation has been requested in Japan to ban organ transplant tourism, and many people have contributed by spreading information about the atrocities in China. The film screening emphasized that it is important not to be indifferent to the problem of forced organ harvesting, and everyone should do what they can. It was brought up that it’s crucial that more people pay attention to the issue and take action to promote a resolution to the problem through information dissemination and signature collection campaigns.

The CCP’s forced organ harvesting was first revealed by witnesses who fled China, and investigations were carried based on their testimonies. Several films have been produced to expose these egregious human rights violations. Although there are few witnesses and evidence is difficult to obtain, many people have put forth great effort to make sure that this issue is not forgotten.


Mr. David Matas

During the film and ensuing discussion, it was made clear that the issue of forced organ harvesting in China is an organized crime in which the state participates, targeting prisoners of conscience—mainly Falun Gong practitioners. The international community has expressed serious concern about what is happening in China.

The testimonies of Falun Gong practitioners play an important role in exposing forced organ harvesting. The testimonies indicate that Falun Gong practitioners underwent blood tests in prison, and many practitioners have simply disappeared. This organ transplant compatibility testing is ongoing, and Falun Gong practitioners’ biodata is included in a massive databank. Those who are selected as organ donors are killed as part of the process of organ transplantation.

I honestly think this movie isn't going to be as simple as the pied piper. A couple things I noticed from the trailer. Starting at 1:28, it shows kids basically breaking out of that house in a way that's not natural. They break through windows without fear of getting hurt, that's why I initially thought of something like AI or something like that. Either way this house is extremely important to the story as they show it at least 4 times and is the house I'm assuming the teacher circles on the map. Benedict Wong's character is the one breaking the window out and his face is red as if he has been attacked or is also controlled somehow. The kids completely act as if they are being controlled and then they come back and essentially make their parents kill themselves in brutal ways. At 1:30, you see someone stepping over what looks like a salt line. That to me says witchcraft possibly. It's the main takeaway I have from the trailer. The kids are taken and sent back as weapons or they are already weapons and they were recalled back to that house as there is something wrong with that specific model. I.E. They are basically designed to look and act like humans but they are really not human at all and are weapons. All complete guesses. And finally, at 1:40, that character is played by Toby Huss. The narrator from the teaser trailer. In drag.
